ocr.text: The following data table presents a comprehensive analysis of predictors associated with the presence of gallbladder pathology
in patients diagnosed with Primary Sclerosing Cholangitis (PSC) and undergoing percutaneous transluminal balloon dilation
(PPI). Notably, the table includes vital statistical metrics such as the odds ratio (OR) and the p-value, which are critical for
assessing the significance of the predictors in question. For instance, the OR of 0.97 suggests that the likelihood of gallbladder
pathology is slightly decreased in patients with GP within the submucosa or sphincter of Oddi layer compared to those
exceeding these anatomical boundaries.
Predictor  χ2  df  p  OR  95% CI  R2  Sensitivity  Specificity  Accuracy  PPV  NPV  AUC
GP within the submucosa or sphincter of Oddi layer  GP exceeding the submucosa or sphincter of Oddi layer  Statistical analysis
Number of patients  81  72
Age (years), range  16–84 (median, 50.0)  15–73 (median, 57.0)  Significant difference was found(Mann–Whitney U test, P = 0.016)
Tumor size (mm), range  5.5–65 (median, 21.0)  8–100 (median, 20.0)  No significant difference was found(Mann–Whitney U test, P = 0.175)
Sex(male to female)  55 : 26  36 : 36  Significant difference was found(χ2 test, P = 0.032)
Rate of lymph node metastasis  7.4% (6/81)  20.8% (15/72)  Significant difference was found(χ2 test, P = 0.019)
Risk group  # of observations  % Rapid progressors  Average risk score
Additionally, the p-value of 0.00 indicates a statistically significant association, warranting further investigation. The analysis
encompasses a total of 81 patients, with 72 presenting varying degrees of gallbladder involvement, thus providing a robust
dataset for evaluating the clinical implications of gallbladder pathology in this specific cohort. The insights gleaned from this
study are critical for guiding therapeutic decisions and improving patient outcomes in individuals with PSC.
